[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Sarracenia_2.jpg/250px-Sarracenia_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sarracenia_2.jpg)

Many *[Sarracenia](/wiki/Sarracenia "Sarracenia")* hybrids are easy to grow.

In horticulture, carnivorous plants are considered a curiosity or a rarity, but are becoming more common in cultivation with the advent of mass-production tissue-culture propagation techniques. [Venus flytraps](/wiki/Venus_flytraps "Venus flytraps") are still the most commonly grown, usually available at garden centers and hardware stores, sometimes offered alongside other easy-to-grow varieties. Nurseries that specialise in growing carnivorous plants exclusively also exist, more uncommon or demanding varieties of carnivorous plants can be obtained from specialist nurseries. [California Carnivores](/wiki/California_Carnivores "California Carnivores") is a notable example of such a nursery in the US that specialises in the cultivation of carnivorous plants. It is owned and operated by horticulturalist [Peter D'Amato](/wiki/Peter_D%27Amato "Peter D'Amato").[[72]](#cite_note-Adams-2013-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) [Rob Cantley](/wiki/Rob_Cantley "Rob Cantley")'s [Borneo Exotics](/wiki/Borneo_Exotics "Borneo Exotics") in [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") is a large nursery that sells worldwide.[[74]](#cite_note-Fernando-2011-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Although different species of carnivorous plants have different cultivation requirements in terms of sunlight, humidity, soil moisture, etc., there are commonalities. Most carnivorous plants require rainwater, or water that has been [distilled](/wiki/Distill "Distill") or deionised by [reverse osmosis](/wiki/Reverse_osmosis "Reverse osmosis").[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77) Common tap or drinking water contains minerals (particularly [calcium](/wiki/Calcium "Calcium") salts) that will quickly build up and kill the plant.[[78]](#cite_note-78) This is because most carnivorous plants have evolved in nutrient-poor, acidic soils and are consequently extreme [calcifuges](/wiki/Calcifuge "Calcifuge"). They are therefore very sensitive to excessive soil-borne nutrients. Since most of these plants are found in [bogs](/wiki/Bog "Bog"), almost all are very intolerant of drying. There are exceptions:
tuberous sundews require a dry (summer) [dormancy](/wiki/Estivation "Estivation") period, and *Drosophyllum* requires much drier conditions than most.

Outdoor-grown carnivorous plants generally catch more than enough insects to keep themselves properly fed. Insects may be fed to the plants by hand to supplement their diet; however, carnivorous plants are generally unable to digest large non-insect food items; bits of hamburger, for example, will simply rot, and this may cause the trap, or even the whole plant, to die.

A carnivorous plant that catches no insects at all will rarely die, although its growth may be impaired. In general, these plants are best left to their own devices: after underwatering with tap-water, the most common cause of Venus flytrap death is prodding the traps to watch them close and feeding them inappropriate items.

Most carnivorous plants require bright light, and most will look better under such conditions, as this encourages them to synthesise red and purple [anthocyanin](/wiki/Anthocyanin "Anthocyanin") pigments, (or [betalain](/wiki/Betalain "Betalain") pigments within Caryophyllales). *Nepenthes* and *Pinguicula* will do better out of full sun, but most other species are happy in direct sunlight.

Carnivores mostly live in bogs, and those that do not are generally tropical. Hence, most require high humidity. On a small scale, this can be achieved by placing the plant in a wide saucer containing pebbles that are kept permanently wet. Small *Nepenthes* species grow well in large [terraria](/wiki/Terrarium "Terrarium").

Many carnivores are native to cold temperate regions and can be grown outside in a bog garden year-round. Most *Sarracenia* can tolerate temperatures well below freezing, despite most species being native to the southeastern United States. Species of *Drosera* and *Pinguicula* also tolerate subfreezing temperatures. *Nepenthes* species, which are tropical, require temperatures from 20 to 30 °C (70 to 90°F) to thrive.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/2005-12-18_N_rajah_034.jpg/250px-2005-12-18_N_rajah_034.jpg)](/wiki/File:2005-12-18_N_rajah_034.jpg)

Cultivated *[Nepenthes rajah](/wiki/Nepenthes_rajah "Nepenthes rajah")* and a few other species.

Carnivorous plants require appropriate nutrient-poor soil. Most appreciate a 3:1 mixture of *[Sphagnum](/wiki/Sphagnum "Sphagnum")* peat to sharp horticultural sand ([coir](/wiki/Coir "Coir") is an acceptable, and more ecofriendly substitute for peat). *Nepenthes* will grow in orchid compost or in pure *[Sphagnum](/wiki/Sphagnum "Sphagnum")* moss.

Carnivorous plants are themselves susceptible to infestation by parasites such as [aphids](/wiki/Aphids "Aphids") or [mealybugs](/wiki/Mealybug "Mealybug"). Although small infestations can be removed by hand, larger infestations necessitate use of an [insecticide](/wiki/Insecticide "Insecticide").

[Isopropyl alcohol](/wiki/Isopropyl_alcohol "Isopropyl alcohol") (rubbing alcohol) is effective as a topical insecticide, particularly on [scale insects](/wiki/Scale_insect "Scale insect"). [Diazinon](/wiki/Diazinon "Diazinon") is an excellent systemic insecticide that is tolerated by most carnivorous plants. [Malathion](/wiki/Malathion "Malathion") and [Acephate](/wiki/Acephate "Acephate") ([Orthene](/wiki/Orthene "Orthene")) have also been reported as tolerable by carnivorous plants.

Although insects can be a problem, by far the biggest killer of carnivorous plants (besides human maltreatment) is [grey mold](/wiki/Botrytis_cinerea "Botrytis cinerea") (*Botrytis cinerea*). This thrives under warm, humid conditions and can be a real problem in winter. To some extent, temperate carnivorous plants can be protected from this pathogen by ensuring that they are kept cool and well ventilated in winter and that any dead leaves are removed promptly. If this fails, a [fungicide](/wiki/Fungicide "Fungicide") is in order.

The easiest carnivorous plants for beginners are those from the cool temperate zone. These plants will do well under cool greenhouse conditions (minimum 5 °C; 40°F in winter, maximum 25 °C; 75°F in summer) if kept in wide trays of acidified or rain water during summer and kept moist during winter:

* *[Drosera capensis](/wiki/Drosera_capensis "Drosera capensis")*, the Cape sundew: attractive strap-leaved sundew, pink flowers, very tolerant of maltreatment.
* *[Drosera binata](/wiki/Drosera_binata "Drosera binata")*, the fork-leaved sundew: large, *Y*-shaped leaves.
* *[Sarracenia flava](/wiki/Sarracenia_flava "Sarracenia flava")*, the yellow trumpet pitcher: yellow, attractively veined leaves, yellow flowers in spring.
* *[Pinguicula grandiflora](/wiki/Pinguicula_grandiflora "Pinguicula grandiflora")*, the common butterwort: purple flowers in spring, hibernates as a bud ([hibernaculum](/wiki/Hibernaculum_(botany) "Hibernaculum (botany)")) in winter. Fully hardy.
* *[Pinguicula moranensis](/wiki/Pinguicula_moranensis "Pinguicula moranensis")*, the Mexican butterwort: pink flowers, non-carnivorous leaves in winter.

Venus flytraps will do well under these conditions but are actually rather difficult to grow: even if treated well, they will often succumb to grey mold in winter unless well ventilated. Some of the lowland *Nepenthes* are very easy to grow as long as they are provided with relatively constant, hot and humid conditions.

## Medicinal uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnivorous_plant&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Medicinal uses")]

A study published in 2009 by researchers from [Tel Aviv University](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_University "Tel Aviv University") indicates that secretions produced by carnivorous plants contain compounds that have anti-fungal properties and may lead to the development of a new class of [anti-fungal drugs](/wiki/Anti-fungal_drug "Anti-fungal drug") that will be effective against [infections](/wiki/Mycosis "Mycosis") that are resistant to current anti-fungal drugs.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-Israel_21c_Innovation_News_Service-2010-80)

## Cultural depictions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnivorous_plant&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Cultural depictions")]

In 1789, [Erasmus Darwin](/wiki/Erasmus_Darwin "Erasmus Darwin") described *Drosera* in the second part of his poem *[The Botanic Garden](/wiki/The_Botanic_Garden "The Botanic Garden")*:[[81]](#cite_note-Schaefer-2021-81)

> "Queen of the marsh, imperial Drosera treads
>
> Rush-fringed banks, and moss-embroider'd beds; ...  
> Bright shines the silver halo, as she turns;
>
> And, as she steps, the living lustre burns.[[81]](#cite_note-Schaefer-2021-81)

However, Erasmus Darwin and others of his generation assumed that the "wonderful contrivance[s]" of carnivorous plants were solely defense mechanisms to "prevent various insects from plundering the honey, or devouring the seed". They realized that the plants were killing insects, but did not understand why. Erasmus Darwin's grandson, [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin"), and great-grandson, [Francis Darwin](/wiki/Francis_Darwin "Francis Darwin"), spent many years studying carnivorous plants. Charles Darwin recognized and described the significance of plant carnivory for nutrition.[[81]](#cite_note-Schaefer-2021-81)[[12]](#cite_note-Hedrich-2021-12)

In 1860, residents of [Providence](/wiki/Providence,_Rhode_Island "Providence, Rhode Island"), [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"), dug up the grave of that state's founder [Roger Williams](/wiki/Roger_Williams "Roger Williams"), intending to move his remains to a new memorial in his honor. They found only teeth, nails, bone fragments, and an apple tree root that had grown along where his body had been, forking midway to follow his legs. The now-severed root forms its own sort of memorial, and has been called "The tree (or root) that ate Roger Williams".[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/The_ya-te-veo.jpg/250px-The_ya-te-veo.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_ya-te-veo.jpg)

Depiction of a native being consumed by a Yateveo ("I see you") carnivorous tree of Central America, from *Land and Sea* by J.W. Buel, 1887

Possibly the earliest published account of a [man-eating plant](/wiki/Man-eating_plant "Man-eating plant") was a literary fabrication that first appeared in 1874. The story of *Crinoida dajeeana*, also known as the Devil Tree of Madagascar or Man-Eating Tree of Madagascar, first appeared in the daily edition of the *[New York World](/wiki/New_York_World "New York World")* on 26 April 1874, and again in the weekly edition two days later.[[85]](#cite_note-85) It purported to be from a German [explorer](/wiki/Explorer "Explorer") named "Karl Leche" (also spelled as Karl or Carl Liche in later accounts), who described seeing a woman fed to a tree as a [sacrifice](/wiki/Human_sacrifice "Human sacrifice") by the "little known but cruel" "Mkodo tribe" of [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar").[[86]](#cite_note-Sullivan-2007-86)
Authorship of the fantastical story would later be attributed by Frederick Maxwell Somers to one Edmund Spencer in the August 1888 issue of the magazine *Current Literature*.[[87]](#cite_note-Somers-1888-87)[[88]](#cite_note-Spencer-1888-88) The story was reprinted widely, appearing as far away as the *[South Australian Register](/wiki/South_Australian_Register "South Australian Register")* in 1881, where it was accompanied by an illustration of a tree consuming a woman. The account has been debunked as pure myth, and Dr. Liche, the Mkodos, and the tree itself were all fabrications.[[86]](#cite_note-Sullivan-2007-86)

*Crinoida dajeeana* notwithstanding, carnivorous plants are credited with widely entering the popular imagination through the nonfiction publications of [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin").[[7]](#cite_note-Pain-2022-7)[[10]](#cite_note-Nature-2017-10) *[Insectivorous Plants](/wiki/Insectivorous_Plants_(book) "Insectivorous Plants (book)")* (1875), followed by *[The Power of Movement in Plants](/wiki/The_Power_of_Movement_in_Plants "The Power of Movement in Plants")* (1880), challenged the idea of a what a plant was and what it was capable of doing, and inspired authors like [Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Arthur Conan Doyle") to imagine enormous and sometimes mobile man-eaters.[[7]](#cite_note-Pain-2022-7) Doyle modeled the sticky end of a character in "The American's Tale" (1880) on a venus flytrap.[[89]](#cite_note-89) [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells") imagined a tentacular blood-sucking plant in "The Flowering of the Strange Orchid" (1894).[[90]](#cite_note-Janzen-2016-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91)

Since then, carnivorous plants have been the subject of popular interest and exposition, much of it highly inaccurate. Typically, these fictional depictions include exaggerated characteristics, such as enormous size or possession of abilities beyond the realm of reality, and can be viewed as a kind of [artistic license](/wiki/Artistic_license "Artistic license").
In a 1939 pamphlet on carnivorous plants written for the [Field Museum](/wiki/Field_Museum "Field Museum"), Sophia Prior recounts the Man-Eating Tree of Madagascar and other "stories of vegetable monsters". She dismisses them all as fables, and notes that they are invariably set in locales that are "indefinite" and "difficult of access".[[92]](#cite_note-Time-1939-92)[[93]](#cite_note-Prior-1939-93)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Little_Shop_of_Horrors_gore.JPG/250px-Little_Shop_of_Horrors_gore.JPG)](/wiki/File:Little_Shop_of_Horrors_gore.JPG)

Audrey Jr., the man-eating plant from [cult film](/wiki/Cult_film "Cult film") *[The Little Shop of Horrors](/wiki/The_Little_Shop_of_Horrors "The Little Shop of Horrors")*

Fictional carnivorous plants have been featured in books, movies, television series, and video games. Some, such as the [mockumentary](/wiki/Mockumentary "Mockumentary") *[The Hellstrom Chronicle](/wiki/The_Hellstrom_Chronicle "The Hellstrom Chronicle")* (1971), use accurate depictions of carnivorous plants for cinematic purposes, while others depend more heavily on imagination.[[10]](#cite_note-Nature-2017-10)[[81]](#cite_note-Schaefer-2021-81)[[94]](#cite_note-Lillemose-2015-94)[[95]](#cite_note-Rice-2018-95)[[96]](#cite_note-Ryder-2020-96)[[97]](#cite_note-Souter-2021-97)

Two of the most famous examples of fictional carnivorous plants in popular culture are the [triffids](/wiki/Triffid "Triffid") of [John Wyndham](/wiki/John_Wyndham_(writer) "John Wyndham (writer)")'s 1951 novel *[The Day of the Triffids](/wiki/The_Day_of_the_Triffids "The Day of the Triffids")* and Audrey Jr./II, the man-eating plant in the 1960s [black comedy](/wiki/Black_comedy "Black comedy") *[The Little Shop of Horrors](/wiki/The_Little_Shop_of_Horrors "The Little Shop of Horrors")* and its subsequent [stage musical adaptation](/wiki/Little_Shop_of_Horrors_(musical) "Little Shop of Horrors (musical)").[[81]](#cite_note-Schaefer-2021-81)

Carnivorous flowers appear in one of the episodes of Zdenek Miler's famous Czech cartoon, *[The Little Mole](/wiki/The_Little_Mole "The Little Mole")* (Krtek a medicína / The Little Mole and Medicine, 1987). Here, the little mole is in Africa, and the carnivorous flowers swallow a fly. The little mole also gets his hand caught, but he is unable to pull it out. The little mole tries to "feed" the carnivorous flower an incomprehensible stone in revenge, but the flower is "intelligent" and spits the stone back out.[[98]](#cite_note-98)
